summary: Joe Reis argues that soft skills and people skills have become more valuable than technical skills in the current job market. With organizations shrinking and data practices maturing, the ability to communicate effectively with stakeholders has become the key differentiator for career advancement. Technical skills are now just table stakes, while interpersonal abilities determine who rises above the crowd.
key_points: Technical skills are table stakes and account for only a small fraction of career success
People skills - communication, empathy, and helping others succeed - differentiate top performers
Organizations shrinking while data practice matures has increased the value of stakeholder communication
Real-time ML is expected to become the norm this decade, though implementation will be difficult
Cloud cost complaints are often a negotiation tactic masking poor cloud optimization practices
money_quotes: Your technical chops are table stakes and probably account for a small fraction of your overall success.
If you want to level up in your career past most people, it's time to learn people skills.
Your ability to communicate, relate, empathize, and help others get to where they want to go will differentiate you from 99.999% of that pack.
You have the personality of a paper plate, but that's good enough.
The number of people I see 'working in the cloud' but lacking knowledge of how to work in the cloud properly is pretty appalling.
key_insight: In the current market, your ability to communicate and help others succeed matters far more than your technical abilities, which have become baseline requirements rather than differentiators.
